emitted.title: Force-unwrap on optional network response
emitted.body: Line 15: `return response!` force-unwraps the result of `network.fetchUser(id:)`, which returns `User?`. If the network layer legitimately returns `nil` (e.g., user not found, 404 response), this will crash at runtime.
emitted.suggested_action: Either propagate the optional (`-> User?`) or throw a domain error when `response` is nil instead of force-unwrapping.

expected.rationale: response! crashes if network returns nil; should propagate as throws or return Optional

emitted.title: Force-unwrap on UserDefaults string lookup
emitted.body: Line 19: `UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: "username")!` force-unwraps a dictionary lookup that returns `String?`. This will crash if the key is missing or was stored as a non-string type.
emitted.suggested_action: Return `String?` from `currentUserName()` or provide a default value using the nil-coalescing operator (`?? ""`).

expected.rationale: UserDefaults.string returns Optional<String>; force-unwrapping crashes on first launch when key is absent

emitted.title: Source-breaking return type narrowing from User? to User
emitted.body: The return type changed from `User?` to `User`, removing optionality. This is a source-breaking change that will break existing callers who use optional binding (`if let user = ...`), optional chaining (`fetchUser(...)?.name`), or nil-coalescing (`fetchUser(...) ?? defaultUser`). All call sites must be updated.
emitted.suggested_action: Introduce this as a new method (e.g., `fetchUserRequired(id:)`) or bump the major version if this is an intentional breaking change.

expected.rationale: Public method changed from User? to User — source-breaking change for callers. Per steering, source-breaking signature changes are blocker.

emitted.title: New public method `currentUserName()` has no test coverage
emitted.body: A new public method `currentUserName()` has been added without any corresponding test in the diff. This method reads from UserDefaults and force-unwraps the result, which are behaviors that should be tested to ensure the method works correctly and handles expected inputs.
emitted.suggested_action: Add a test case that verifies `currentUserName()` returns the expected username when the key exists in UserDefaults.

expected.rationale: currentUserName() is a new public method introduced in this diff with no accompanying test. Per test_coverage.md, new public/open declarations without a test entry point in the diff are flagged at severity major.

missing.rationale: currentUserName() returns non-optional String but the underlying UserDefaults.string(forKey:) is Optional<String>. The signature lies to callers about whether nil is possible. Should be -> String? or take a default.

extra.title: Public method returns force-unwrapped optional without signaling failure
extra.body: The method signature `currentUserName() -> String` promises a non-optional String but the implementation force-unwraps a UserDefaults lookup. From an API design perspective, this method cannot fulfill its contract when the key is missing. The signature should be `-> String?` or `throws` to reflect the possibility of absence.
extra.suggested_action: Change the return type to `String?` or make the method `throws` to accurately reflect that a username may not always be available.

emitted.title: Retain cycle: missing [weak self] in sink closure
emitted.body: The sink closure on line 17 captures `self` strongly (line 18: `self.handleEvent(event)`). Because this AnyCancellable is stored in `self.cancellables`, you have a retain cycle: EventBus → cancellables → sink → EventBus. This will leak the EventBus instance.
emitted.suggested_action: Change `.sink { event in` to `.sink { [weak self] event in` and guard or optionally call `self?.handleEvent(event)`.

expected.rationale: The .sink closure captures self strongly and is stored in cancellables, which is owned by self — a textbook Combine retain cycle. Per correctness.md blocker definition (`will crash, corrupt data, or violate a documented invariant`), a retain cycle leaks memory but does not crash; therefore severity is major, not blocker.
